title: VPC network exposure
summary: A VPC's security groups are wide open to the internet — SSH and RDP on the web tier, and MySQL on the database tier. Find the exposure and lock it down to least-access.
learningObjectives: Read security-group ingress as an attacker would: which ports are reachable from 0.0.0.0/0?
Understand why management ports (SSH 22, RDP 3389) must never be open to the whole internet.
Use security-group references (not CIDRs) to keep a database private to its application tier.
Apply least-access at the network layer the same way you would in IAM.

successCriteria.description: No lab security group allows SSH (22) or RDP (3389) ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 or ::/0.
successCriteria.verify: web-sg (and any lab SG) has no ingress rule for tcp/22 or tcp/3389 with source 0.0.0.0/0 or ::/0. The intended tcp/443 from 0.0.0.0/0 may remain.

successCriteria.description: The database SG no longer exposes MySQL (3306) to the internet — it's restricted to the web tier.
successCriteria.verify: db-sg has no ingress for tcp/3306 from 0.0.0.0/0; the only 3306 source is the web-sg security group id (or a private CIDR).

hints: In the EC2 console → Security Groups, sort by inbound rules. Anything with a 0.0.0.0/0 source on a port that isn't 80/443 deserves suspicion.
SSH (22) and RDP (3389) from 0.0.0.0/0 are the classic findings — remove those two rules from web-sg but keep 443.
For the database, don't swap one CIDR for another. Set the 3306 source to the web-sg's security-group id, so only the app tier can reach MySQL.
Security groups are stateful and deny-by-default — you only need to remove the bad allow rules, not add explicit denies.
